_note: Original Practice questions aligned with CS Navigator's original Learn lesson for this topic.

questions.title: DOM & Events practice 1
questions.prompt: What can `querySelector` return when no element matches?
choices: null
An empty string
false only
A new element
questions.explanation: The method returns null when it cannot find a matching element, so code should check before accessing properties.

questions.title: DOM & Events practice 2
questions.prompt: When does a click listener run?
choices: After the registered element receives a click
As soon as it is registered
Before the page loads every time
Only when the function returns text
questions.explanation: Registration stores the callback for later; the browser invokes it when the matching click event occurs.

questions.title: Trace DOM & Events
questions.prompt: What output does this code produce? Enter each line in order.
accepted: The displayed number increases after each click.
questions.explanation: The setup registers one listener. Each click then updates the stored count and the page text. The resulting output is 'The displayed number increases after each click.'.

questions.title: DOM & Events practice 5
choices: querySelector returns null when nothing matches, and reading null.textContent throws
textContent is read-only and cannot be assigned
querySelector returns an array that must be indexed
The guard makes the selector run faster
questions.explanation: When no element matches, `querySelector` returns `null`, and `null.textContent = ...` throws a TypeError. The guard runs the assignment only when an element was found. `querySelector` returns a single element (not an array), `textContent` is writable, and the check is about safety, not speed.

questions.title: DOM & Events concept check 1
questions.prompt: What does the lesson mean by "Use textContent for plain text"?
choices: An empty string
`innerHTML` asks the browser to parse markup. Use `textContent` for ordinary user-facing text, especially when a value came from input, so text is displayed rather than treated as page structure.
false only
A new element
questions.explanation: The DOM represents the page as objects. Select an element, verify it exists, then register an event listener. That reasoning supports the answer: `innerHTML` asks the browser to parse markup. Use `textContent` for ordinary user-facing text, especially when a value came from input, so text is displayed rather than treated as page structure.

questions.title: DOM & Events concept check 2
questions.prompt: Which choice describes a reliable DOM & Events practice?
choices: As soon as it is registered
Before the page loads every time
Select the intended element precisely.
Only when the function returns text
questions.explanation: The DOM represents the page as objects. Select an element, verify it exists, then register an event listener. That reasoning supports the answer: Select the intended element precisely.

questions.title: DOM & Events concept check 3
questions.prompt: Which recommendation belongs in a review of DOM & Events code?
choices: document.querySelector("#missing").textContent = "Ready";
A new element
As soon as it is registered
Check a selector result before use.
questions.explanation: The DOM represents the page as objects. Select an element, verify it exists, then register an event listener. That reasoning supports the answer: Check a selector result before use.

questions.title: DOM & Events concept check 4
questions.prompt: Which guideline helps prevent a common DOM & Events mistake?
choices: Keep listeners short and focused.
Before the page loads every time
Only when the function returns text
document.querySelector("#missing").textContent = "Ready";
questions.explanation: The DOM represents the page as objects. Select an element, verify it exists, then register an event listener. That reasoning supports the answer: Keep listeners short and focused.

questions.title: DOM & Events concept check 5
questions.prompt: A classmate is checking their understanding of DOM & Events. Which advice is correct?
choices: An empty string
Display untrusted text with textContent.
false only
Before the page loads every time
questions.explanation: The DOM represents the page as objects. Select an element, verify it exists, then register an event listener. That reasoning supports the answer: Display untrusted text with textContent.

questions.title: DOM & Events concept check 6
questions.prompt: What is the main idea behind DOM & Events?
choices: Only when the function returns text
document.querySelector("#missing").textContent = "Ready";
Connect JavaScript behavior to page elements and user actions.
An empty string
questions.explanation: The DOM represents the page as objects. Select an element, verify it exists, then register an event listener. That reasoning supports the answer: Connect JavaScript behavior to page elements and user actions.
